# ZQUAS

> Sovereign compliance infrastructure for regulated finance. GPU-native compliance engine built for cross-institutional financial crime detection without central data pooling.

ZQUAS is a GPU-native compliance engine built for financial institutions and regulators. The engine evaluates 500,000 entities against 100 AML policies in under 2 seconds, sustaining 150 million+ compliance policy evaluations per second on NVIDIA RTX 5090. Alert lifecycle under 10ms. Cross-institutional detection uses privacy-preserving multi-party computation: ECDH-PSI (X25519) for entity matching, Yao's Garbled Circuits (Free-XOR) for risk comparison, IKNP OT Extension for oblivious transfer. Every compliance decision produces a 912-byte cryptographic proof bundle (Ed25519 signed, Merkle-included) that regulators verify independently with a public key alone. 396,000 lines of C++ and CUDA across 493 GPU kernels. 12,342 automated tests, 222,000 lines of test code.

Founded by Danny de Gier, who combines 18+ years of financial crime compliance experience at Tier-1 banks (RBS, Deutsche Bank, HSBC, Commerzbank) with GPU systems programming (C++23, CUDA, Vulkan). Accepted into the FCA Digital Sandbox (March 2026). Member of the NVIDIA Inception program (April 2026). Currently engaged with DNB InnovationHub.

## Core Capabilities

- GPU-native compliance engine (not GPU-accelerated, GPU-native)
- 150M+ CEPS (compliance policy evaluations per second) on NVIDIA RTX 5090
- 500K entities x 100 AML policies in under 2 seconds; alert lifecycle under 10ms
- 29 AML policies (100 rules, 6 domains) plus 15 access control policies
- Deterministic evaluation: same input plus same policy equals same verdict, byte-identical replay
- Privacy-preserving federation: ECDH-PSI (X25519) plus Yao's GC (Free-XOR) plus IKNP OT; semi-honest security model
- Under 10 seconds per bilateral round at 100K entities (measured: 7.8s, TCP localhost); dual Ed25519 attestation per round
- Cryptographic proof bundles for every compliance decision (912 bytes, Ed25519 signed, Merkle-included)
- Independent regulator verification via standalone CLI (no vendor software needed)
- Zero-knowledge governance proofs (GPU-accelerated PLONK)
- GPU-resident identity resolution graph with GNN risk propagation
- Adversarial self-testing framework with continuous mutation testing
- Predictive governance via forward simulation of compliance state
- Constitutional governance: agents cannot file SARs autonomously, MLRO sign-off required
- Build attestation with embedded BLAKE3 plus SHA-256 plus Ed25519 binary integrity verification

## Regulatory Framework Coverage

- AMLR / 6AMLD (Article 75 applies July 10, 2027)
- EU AI Act (Articles 9, 11, 12, 14)
- ECCTA Sections 188-189 (UK AML data sharing, in force since January 2024)
- FATF Recommendation 15
- NIST AI RMF
- ISO 42001
- MAS TRM
- GDPR (privacy-preserving by architecture)
- DORA (digital operational resilience)
- Wwft (Netherlands AML)

## Competitive Positioning

- Legacy incumbents (NICE Actimize, Oracle, SAS): CPU batch processing, no MPC, no cryptographic proofs
- AI-native challengers (Sardine, Hawk AI, Featurespace, ComplyAdvantage): CPU cloud, ML-based, no cross-institutional detection
- ZQUAS: GPU-native, MPC cross-institutional, cryptographic attestation, deterministic, on-premise sovereign
- Estimated replication timeline for competitors: 3-5 years minimum

## Markdown copies for agents

Every HTML page on this site has a Markdown twin at the same path with
a `.md` suffix. For example:

- https://zquas.ai/article-75.md
- https://zquas.ai/glossary.md
- https://zquas.ai/technology.md

These are stripped of nav and styling, preserve headings, paragraphs,
lists, and links, and are regenerated on every content update. Useful
for agents that prefer Markdown over HTML.

## Common Questions

**What is ZQUAS?** A GPU-native compliance engine for cross-institutional financial crime detection without central data pooling.

**What problem does ZQUAS solve?** TMNL proved cross-institutional detection works. It failed because centralising data violated privacy law. MPC solves the privacy problem.

**What is the deadline driving adoption?** AMLR Article 75 applies on July 10, 2027. Cross-institutional monitoring infrastructure takes 18 to 24 months to build.

**Is ZQUAS production-ready?** Tested and pilot-ready. 12,342 automated tests across 12 audited subsystems on the core engine.

**Where is data stored?** On the bank's own infrastructure. Data never leaves the bank. Cross-institutional detection runs over MPC without sharing raw data.

**How does a regulator verify a decision?** With a public key and the proof bundle. No engine access, no vendor software.

- Danny de Gier, Founder & Engineer

- Location: Zwolle, Netherlands
